analysis:
Across the top-ranked heuristics the common, successful structure is clear: (1) a fast Pareto/nondominated prefilter, (2) a cheap per-point proxy (axis-aligned box volume ± a few-weight scalarization) to build a small candidate shortlist, (3) a CELF-style or greedy selection using cached singleton HVs (and selective pairwise caches) to minimize expensive pg.hypervolume calls, and (4) a bounded local 1-for-1 swap cleanup. Strengths: timestamped heap (CELF) and singleton caches dramatically reduce HV evaluations; pairwise caches are valuable when shortlist ≤ ~40; deterministic seeds and tie-break rules give reproducible results.

Weaker variants either omit lazy evaluation or do many redundant exact HV recomputations (inner loops with vstack + compute), use non-deterministic shuffles/prints, or pick overly large shortlists that blow up HV calls. Common avoidable overheads: repeated creation of small arrays (np.vstack) in tight loops, membership tests against lists instead of sets, O(N^2) Python-level dominance checks instead of vectorized/blocked comparisons, and lack of an explicit HV-call budget or upper-bound pruning (box_vol-based) to early-skip candidates. Good trade-offs seen: smaller, k-proportional shortlists plus upper-bound pruning hit most of the benefit while keeping runtime bounded; heavy-weight many-weight approximations (heuristic 10/11) can approximate HVC well but must be capped to avoid cost/space blowups.

Practical opportunities missed: reuse incremental HV state (where available) or batch multiple candidate evaluations; precompute and reuse ref-diffs; prefer set lookups and preallocated arrays; only compute pairwise HVs when shortlist small; and cap/have a graceful fallback when HV calls approach a budget.
experience: Prioritize a small, k‑proportional shortlist (Pareto-aware), use CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton HVs (add selective pairwise cache when shortlist is small), and prune candidates via cheap box-volume upper bounds. Vectorize dominance and proxy calculations, use sets for membership, limit exact HV calls with an explicit budget, and finish with a bounded deterministic 1‑for‑1 swap. These give reproducible, high‑quality HV subsets with controlled runtime.
current_reflection: Prioritize a small, k‑proportional shortlist (Pareto-aware), use CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton HVs (add selective pairwise cache when shortlist is small), and prune candidates via cheap box-volume upper bounds. Vectorize dominance and proxy calculations, use sets for membership, limit exact HV calls with an explicit budget, and finish with a bounded deterministic 1‑for‑1 swap. These give reproducible, high‑quality HV subsets with controlled runtime.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.
bad_reflections: Use an adaptive shortlist (k- and Pareto-aware), compute cheap HVC approximations (many weight vectors) to rank candidates, run CELF with cached singleton HVs + timestamps, prune with tight box-volume/upper bounds, enforce an HV-call budget, and do a capped candidate-limited deterministic swap pass using incremental HV deltas. This yields reproducible, high-quality subsets with predictable runtime.

Use CELF with cached singletons + selective pairwise HVs, build an adaptive shortlist via moderate-weight scalar HVC and box‑upper bounds, enforce an HV‑call budget, vectorize and reuse buffers, limit targeted swaps, and prefer deterministic seeds. Consider incremental HV updates or parallel HV evaluations to boost exact checks.

Enforce a strict HV‑call budget and use CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton (and selective pairwise) HVs. Build an adaptive, k‑proportional shortlist via a blended box‑volume + few‑weight scalar score, prune with cheap upper bounds, and use O(1) set membership and index-based array views to avoid copying. Add deterministic tie‑breaking and a tiny bounded 1‑for‑1 swap cleanup. Consider parallel HV evaluations or an incremental HV routine if available.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: CELF lazy greedy; Pareto-aware k‑proportional shortlist; cached singleton HVC (+ selective pairwise); box‑volume upper bounds; HV‑call budget; vectorized dominance; incremental HVC deltas; bounded deterministic 1‑for‑1 swap.
- Advice: Prefilter dominated points, build a small shortlist via few-weight scalar HVC + cheap box upper bounds, run CELF with cached singleton HVs (add pairwise cache only when shortlist is tiny), vectorize proxies and use index/set views to avoid copies, enforce an explicit HV-call budget, seed determinism, finish with a bounded deterministic 1‑for‑1 swap to deliver exactly k.
- Avoid: Many exact HV calls, nested recomputations, uncontrolled pairwise caching, nondeterministic tie‑breaking, and needless array copying.
- Explanation: Cheap pruning + cached incremental deltas produce reproducible, high-quality hypervolume subsets with controlled, predictable runtime.
